questions.title: Pipeline stage definitions
questions.text: What are the defined sales pipeline stages, and what business rules govern stage progression (e.g., mandatory activities, qualifying criteria, probability assignments)?

choices.text: Standard opportunity stages (e.g., Lead → Qualified → Proposal → Negotiation → Closed Won/Lost) with fixed probability percentages
Stage progression driven by qualifying criteria or gate activities at each stage with variable probability per deal
Custom stage model specific to our organization (stages and rules defined separately)
All of the above — fixed stages with gate-based progression and probability assignments
Other (please specify)

questions.title: What constitutes a booking
questions.text: How does your organization define a 'booking'? Specifically, which event or milestone marks a deal as booked (e.g., signed contract, revenue recognition, ARR commitment)?

choices.text: Signed contract or agreement — booking is recorded when the contract is executed
Closed Won in CRM — booking is recorded when the opportunity reaches Closed Won stage
Revenue recognized — booking is tied to recognized revenue accounting events
ARR/MRR commitment — booking is measured by annual or monthly recurring revenue commitments
Other (please specify)

questions.title: Customer–segment relationship
questions.text: How does your organization model the relationship between customers and segments (e.g., is segment an attribute of the customer, an account classification, or derived from spend/size)?

choices.text: Segment is a direct attribute on the customer account (e.g., Enterprise, Mid-Market, SMB)
Segment is derived from customer spend, employee count, or other quantitative thresholds
Segment is a territory or geo-based grouping assigned by the sales organization
Multiple segment hierarchies exist (e.g., industry vertical overlaid with size tier)
Other (please specify)

questions.title: Product–business unit relationship
questions.text: How are products organized relative to business units — is each product owned by one business unit, or can products span multiple business units?

choices.text: Each product belongs to exactly one business unit (strict 1-to-N ownership)
Products can belong to multiple business units depending on context (e.g., cross-sell, shared platforms)
Business unit is determined by the selling team, not the product itself
We use a product family → product line → SKU hierarchy with BU mapped at a specific level
Other (please specify)

questions.title: Priority pipeline metrics
questions.text: Which pipeline metrics are most critical for your analysis? Select the primary metric that should drive the skill's focus.

choices.text: Pipeline value and weighted pipeline value (total deal value adjusted by stage probability)
Pipeline velocity (deal age, conversion rates, time-in-stage metrics)
Win rate and loss analysis (win/loss ratios, competitive loss reasons, stage-by-stage conversion)
Bookings attainment (quota vs. actual bookings by period, rep, or territory)
All of the above — the skill should cover all major pipeline and bookings metrics
Other (please specify)

questions.title: Period-over-period comparison needs
questions.text: Does the analysis require period-over-period comparisons, and if so, which temporal grain is most important?

choices.text: Quarter over quarter (fiscal quarters are the primary planning cadence)
Month over month (monthly pipeline reviews are the primary cadence)
Year over year at quarterly grain (seasonal trend analysis dominate)
Comparisons are secondary — the skill should focus on current-state snapshots first
Other (please specify)

questions.title: Stage regression and re-entry
questions.text: How should the skill handle deals that move backward in the pipeline (stage regression) or re-enter the pipeline after being closed lost?

choices.text: Stage regression is rare and can be treated as the deal sitting at the earlier stage
Regression is common and must be modeled explicitly (e.g., date-stamped stage history)
Closed Lost deals that re-open should be treated as new pipeline entries with a fresh lifecycle
Both regression and re-entry are common and need full lifecycle tracking with history
Other (please specify)

questions.title: Multi-product and split-deal handling
questions.text: How should the skill handle a single deal that contains multiple products or spans multiple business units — does each product line become its own line item, or is the deal monolithic?

choices.text: Each product in a deal is a separate line item with its own value, stage, and BU
Deals are monolithic — one value per deal regardless of product mix
Split is optional — some analyses need line-item detail while others aggregate at deal level
Other (please specify)

notes.title: Bookings definition varies across cloud organizations
notes.body: In cloud technology services, 'bookings' can mean signed contract value, ARR commitment, or recognized revenue. This materially affects grain, reconciliation, and modeling decisions. Q2 addresses this directly.

notes.title: Segment hierarchy complexity
notes.body: Customer segment in cloud services is often multi-dimensional (size tier × industry vertical × geo). Q3 probes whether the skill should assume a flat attribute or support hierarchical segmentation.
